opp.next_step: Per the conversation with Dwayne Nicolay, they are open to evaluating Zycus and a meeting has been set for 26th June 2026. They are purely an in-house systems and no proper structure. They are completely running on manual ERP and are looking at CLM and S2C as priority.
opp.ais_why: Cox Automotive has a strong digital transformation and innovation culture, with significant investments in AI, data analytics, automation, and connected automotive solutions. Its procurement organization is likely focused on improving sourcing efficiency, supplier collaboration, contract management, spend visibility, and risk management through AI. Given the company's enterprise scale and technology-first approach, AI-powered Source-to-Pay (S2P), Intake Management, and autonomous procurement capabilities align well with its operational and strategic objectives. Cox Automotive is a strong candidate for advanced procurement AI initiatives.

competition.narrative: No external vendor competitor named; the real competition is 'Do Nothing' / status quo inertia. Dwayne was asked on the 25 Jun call whether he had worked with or come across Zycus before; he said no but asked if Zycus was at Procurecon (indicating early awareness). No other procurement platform (Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP, Zip) was mentioned. Cox is greenfield: 'purely in-house systems, no proper structure', so there is no incumbent to displace. The risk is not a competitive vendor but internal inertia. Cox shut down procurement during COVID and only reopened it a year ago; without a forcing event (audit, compliance mandate, contract renewal), the evaluation could stall or deprioritize. The wedge is to quantify the cost of manual processes (error rate, cycle time, compliance risk) and position Zycus as the structured foundation Cox needs to scale procurement, with Merlin AI as a differentiated capability that manual or legacy ERP cannot match.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain confirmed and owner identified. Cox is running 'completely manual ERP' with 'no proper structure' for CLM or S2C, and is 'building the procurement function from scratch' post-COVID (25 Jun 2026 call, Dwayne Nicolay). The procurement function was shut down during COVID and reopened ~1 year ago; Dwayne (Procurement Manager) took the role to rebuild it. Pain is operational: no centralized contract repository, no supplier management, no structured sourcing process. Dwayne described Cox as 'purely in-house systems' with ad-hoc manual workflows. Pain owner is Dwayne (procurement) with likely escalation to COO or CFO for budget justification. The pain is real and structural, but not yet quantified (no cost of manual errors, cycle-time waste, or compliance risk stated). The risk is 'Do Nothing' inertia: without a compelling event or quantified ROI, the project may deprioritize.

value.detail: Cox is greenfield ('purely in-house systems, no proper structure') and is 'building the procurement function from scratch' post-COVID (per Dwayne, 25 Jun call). Deploying an integrated CLM+S2C+Merlin suite requires Cox to define procurement workflows, train users, and drive adoption across the organization. No mention of internal project management capacity, executive sponsor, or change-management resources on the 25 Jun call. Risk that Cox lacks the capacity to deploy and adopt Zycus without significant Zycus-led implementation support, or that the project stalls in scoping/customization.
